content.text: Avoiding Infinite Loops
An infinite loop in a
useEffect

freezes or crashes the browser tab. It happens when the effect triggers a state change that causes a re-render that triggers the effect again, without end. Understanding the three main causes — no dependency array, unstable object/function references, and self-triggering state — lets you diagnose and fix these bugs quickly.

attrs.alt: Infinite loop cycle: render → effect → setState → re-render, and common fixes

content.text: Cause 1: setState Without a Dependency Array
The simplest infinite loop: a no-array effect calls
setState
. The state change triggers a re-render; the effect fires again on the re-render; it calls
setState
again; and the cycle never ends.
The fix depends on intent. If the effect should run once, add
. If the state update does not belong in an effect at all (e.g., it is computed from props), move it to the render body or
useMemo
Cause 2: Object or Array Literal in the Dependency Array

Object and array literals create a new reference on every render. If such a value is in the dependency array, React sees a "changed" dependency on every render, even if the contents are identical. The effect fires, may trigger state changes or simply re-runs, and the next render creates another new reference.
Stabilise the reference with
useMemo
(for objects/arrays) or move the literal outside the component when it does not depend on any prop or state.
Cause 3: Function Dependency Recreated Every Render
A function defined in the component body is a new reference on every render. If it is listed as a dependency, the effect fires every render.
Use
useCallback
to stabilise the function reference, or move the function definition inside the effect — functions defined inside the effect do not need to be listed as dependencies.
Cause 4: Effect Output Feeds Back Into Its Own Dependency

A subtler loop: the effect reads some state value, derives a new value, and writes it back. If the derived value is always different from what is in state (e.g., adding a timestamp), the write triggers a re-render, which triggers the effect, which writes again.
Use the functional updater form of
setState
when the new value depends on the old value — this avoids reading state as a dependency. Alternatively, compare the new value to the current state before calling
setState
General rule:
if the effect's sole purpose is to transform state and write it back to state, that transformation belongs in
useMemo
during render, not in an effect.
Context Value Creating a Loop
When a context value is an object created inline in the Provider, every render of the Provider creates a new object reference. Any consumer that lists that context value as a dependency in
useEffect
will re-run on every parent render.
Memoize the context value in the Provider with
useMemo
so its reference is stable across renders that do not actually change the content.
Diagnosing an Infinite Loop
React 18 in development will log maximum update depth errors to the console. The error message names the component and the hook triggering the loop. The stack trace points at the
setState
call inside the effect.
A second diagnostic: add
console.log
at the top of the effect. If it logs thousands of times per second, you have a loop. Check each dependency value with
console.log
to find which one changes on every render.
Why Suppressing the Lint Rule Is Wrong

A tempting but wrong fix is to suppress the exhaustive-deps lint warning and manually exclude the cycling dependency from the array. This breaks correctness: the effect will use a stale value of the excluded dep. Fix the reference stability instead.
Using a Ref to Read State Without Dependencies
When an effect needs to read the latest value of a rapidly changing state variable
without
re-running whenever that variable changes, store the value in a ref and read the ref inside the effect. The effect does not close over the state variable, so it does not need it as a dependency.

The first effect keeps the ref synchronised with the state value after every render. The second effect reads the ref — always getting the latest value — without having the state as a dependency. This pattern is common when an interval or timer needs access to current state without resetting on every state change.
Limitation:
the ref approach is appropriate when you need to
read
state in a callback (e.g., inside setInterval). It is not a general replacement for deps — the effect should still list any reactive values it actually depends on for
re-running
useReducer to Consolidate State Updates
When multiple state values interact and the update logic is complex, consolidating them into a
useReducer
often eliminates loops naturally. The dispatch function is stable across renders, so it can be used inside an effect without being listed as a dependency.
dispatch
never changes between renders — React guarantees stability. An effect that calls only
dispatch
and reads no state variables has no state-related dependencies, breaking the common feedback cycle.
Functional Updater to Remove State Dependencies
The functional updater form of
setState
setCount(c => c + 1)
) is a targeted loop-prevention tool. It removes the need to close over the current state value, so the state variable does not need to be listed as a dependency.
In the second example,
count
is not read directly inside the effect — the updater receives it. Therefore,
count
is not a dependency, and the interval does not reset on every count change.
Recognising Loops at a Glance
Before choosing a fix, identify the root cause. This table maps symptoms to causes and fixes:
Symptom
Root cause
Fix
Browser tab freezes immediately
No-array effect + setState
Add [] or useMemo
Effect log repeats on every render
Object/array/function dep identity
useMemo / useCallback / move outside
Interval resets every second
State dep listed for timer effect
Functional updater; remove state dep
Context-consuming effect re-fires constantly
Context value is a new object each render
useMemo in Provider
Derived state written back in effect
Effect output is its own dep
Replace with useMemo computation
Works in prod, loops in dev
Strict Mode reveals missing cleanup
Add cleanup; not a false positive
Stable Dependency Checklist
Before adding a value to a dependency array, verify its identity stability:
Primitive (string, number, boolean, null): always stable if the value itself hasn't changed. Safe to list.
State variable: stable between renders if not updated. Safe to list; it re-runs when value changes.
Prop (primitive): safe to list — only changes when parent passes a different value.
Prop (object/array/function): risky. Re-created by parent on every render unless memoised. Prefer consuming primitive properties.
Local variable (object/array): always unstable. Move outside the component or use useMemo.
Local function: always unstable. Move inside the effect or use useCallback.
Ref (.current): do not list. React does not track ref.current; changes do not trigger re-renders.
dispatch from useReducer: always stable. Safe to omit from deps (lint rules know this).
setState setter: always stable. Safe to omit from deps.
Summary
Infinite loops in
useEffect
are caused by effects triggering the very state changes that caused them to run. Every loop has one of four root causes, each with a structural fix.
No array + setState:
add [] or move the computation to render/useMemo.
Object/array dep:
move stable literals outside the component or stabilise with useMemo.
Function dep:
define the function inside the effect or stabilise with useCallback.
Feedback loop:
if an effect only transforms state and writes it back, use useMemo instead.
Context:
memoize context values in the Provider; consume only primitive deps from context.
Diagnosis:
check React's maximum-update-depth error and log each dep individually.
Lint suppression:
never eslint-disable exhaustive-deps to hide a loop — fix the reference stability instead.
